All right, so welcome everybody. I appreciate seeing where everybody's coming from. Looks like we have so far 53 or so participants. We've scheduled this for a couple of hours. We may go over. The format is going to be people who sent questions first. We'll have priority. And I think it's about seven pages of questions. It's not seven pages of individual questions. There's some background. I don't want to get the names horribly wrong. So for the most part, I'm not going to share the person who asked the question. You'll know your question when you're listening to it. Some of these are people who are not going to be able to be on the call, and others just sort of thought out loud and put together a question. I'm not going to just one caveat about this. A lot of people ask questions for three, four, or five, six weeks down the road. And I'm not going to answer questions about that. I might throw the question out there. And if there's something that now we need to think about, then I'll answer it. But some of you, based on your emails, are trying to write Facebook ad copy now. And I cannot stress enough that you should stop doing that. You're going to compromise what you should be learning now by trying to imagine what you're going to learn five or six weeks from now. When I train students and take one to one of the things that I tell them all the time is, do not try to get through the drill. Be in the drill. Be in the training. So I do understand that many of you are working on your own businesses, and you're trying to sort of project ahead. And I get that, or you're working with clients. I absolutely get that. But if you continue to have your eyes on the horizon at the expense of what we're talking about now, when we actually do get to that point on the horizon, it's just not going to be nearly as valuable. So please do the work in the order that we're doing it. Please spend time in the things that we're doing now, because everything else will make a lot more sense, and it will be a lot more valuable to you when we get to that point. OK, so having said that, I may read a question as we go. I'm going to answer that later. Or I may violate everything I just said and just answer the questions, because who knows? I'm going to start with a question from Mike. Mike is, and this may turn into a hot seat. So Mike, if you're on the call, sort of let Andre know you're here. We can sort of continue. So here's the background from Mike. He says, now I have an opportunity to build my coaching practice. My initial offerings are various reports that help you learn things like behavioral styles, communication styles, work styles, and other styles, depending on the reports. Then I am deciding on how I want the coaching pipeline to work. If I want to do a mastermind with John Maxwell's materials, it's an expert in that space, or some other thing that may be more valuable to my clients. My question for you is this. How much of the back end needs to be developed before we start marketing to our potential clients? I have almost too many options right now, and I'm trying to figure out what is the best way to go. Do I need to decide now, or can I gather information from clients and adapt as I go? You absolutely do not have to have a back end built before you start paid traffic. Actually, the only thing that you really need is to have an idea, and when we get into the testing phase, we can talk about how you can even test an idea. But if you're trying to monetize traffic in the short term, and when I say monetize, just make your money back, or make some portion of your money back, then having something on the front end that you're selling is ideal, or if you're just doing lead gen, then you just need to have a budget. You just need to be able to say, I'll pick a number, and these numbers are not meaningful. They're just example numbers. Mike could be in a situation where he has these reports, he wants to give them away or sell them, depending on what it is, and he wants to know which ones resonate most with his audience, and he is willing to spend $500 to get whatever, 7,500, 200 people onto, into some sort of audience, probably by email, to have that conversation with them. One on one, give them the, let's say it's three reports, and they select a report, and then he's communicated with them by email and asking them questions. What are you looking for? What are you interested in? What's the biggest problem you're trying to solve? Like really engaging with whoever signs up to determine first of all, how receptive are they? If you have 100 people who have requested a report, and two of them reply to your emails, it's not a very responsive audience. That's a very important thing to know. If you send, if you have 100 people in an audience, by you send them emails, personal emails, asking them what they were looking for, and you get 60 responses, and every one of their responses, it's clear they have a very pressing need. Well, that's a very different piece of information. So the overall framework of front end and back end is it's a longer term framework, but we need to know it now primarily so that we understand that the front end of a business, in general, is not where the money is made. That's not always true. I mean, if you are selling a high cost offer, whatever that is, $2,000 program, $18,000 program, and you have no front end, that's fine. You just have a different metric, which is real as return on ad spend, it's just a different metric to use, but you still need to understand the concepts. And that's really why I spent so much time on these distinctions between CPA, AOV, and LTV. Even if you don't actually use any of those numbers, you absolutely have to understand what those numbers would mean conceptually, that you are paying to acquire a customer, and in some period of time, you want to make as much of that money back as possible in a short period of time so that you don't run out of money acquiring customers. And then the actual money, the profit, is made usually after that first sale, but not always, sometimes on that first sale. So long answer to a short question, Mike's gonna jump on here, I think, to follow up with this, but the main question here is how much of the backend do you have to have finished and you don't, but don't use that as an excuse, or don't use that as permission to just go do whatever on the front end, having no idea how you're going to monetize it later. Like I would be doing a terrible disservice to you if I said, oh, don't even think about it. So what we're really trying to do here is to put the, I don't wanna say the least amount of time and the least amount of money, because that suggests that we're not really committing, but we want to put an appropriate amount of time and an appropriate amount of ad spend to answer a specific question. And that question might be broad, like is there really a market here? And then the way that you get that information, part of its science, and we've all been, there's sort of, there's a myth about how scientific all of this really is, that there's so many leaks and tracking and there's so many, sort of just broad leaks that as much as we want this to be decimal place accuracy, it's okay to have anecdotal information, especially at first, because what you're really doing is you're saying, I think, I being you, that these three reports of the highest value to this particular audience, because I think this is the need that it addresses, but the moment you get some, you have the ability to test that hypothesis, like you have 10, 12, 15 people who have received those reports, that's when you want to start those conversations. And what you're really trying to figure out is were you at all right in your assessment? Like you might have thought that, the biggest issue that they're struggling is X, and you get on the phone with them, you realize that's not the issue that they're struggling with at all, it's why. And then that allows you to course correct, and that back and forth and back and forth is what builds, really, it builds such a solid understanding of your audience, which is better for your audience, which is in turn better for you. So how you do that, emails one way. A popular way is through surveys. I don't love surveys, just, I don't know what it is about them. I just, I've worked with a lot of clients, you've used them, I don't know, it's fascinating to me, but the survey to me is not as immediate as having somebody reply to an email or say, yeah, I'd love to do a 15 minute Skype call with you. And people will, I mean, if you can do that, make that offer. But have those conversations, it's for this idea of getting outside the building and really talking to customers, while the building might be your home office, whatever. Get outside and ask people, and really engage with them. And listen, that's the other thing. Like you're not looking for evidence to confirm, this is probably the biggest mistake I've seen happen, is somebody has an idea, they solicit feedback, and what they mean by soliciting feedback is they're looking for confirmation that their original idea was right, and as soon as they have enough of that confirmation, they check the box and they move forward with that idea. Don't do that. Ask the questions and then listen to what people have to say. And then when you begin the second round, whatever that is a week later, two weeks later, the language of your advertising will change, the language of your, all of your messaging will change, maybe slightly, but it will change for the better because now it's informed by what your audience has told you really matters to them. Does that make sense? Yeah, that's awesome. Thank you, because I mean, that's my biggest fear is I have this abstract idea, but what is the reality? And I mean, I've obviously, I've talked to people about this, but for whatever reason, I didn't really think about that in a general faceless, the people I know who I have put through this process already, I can solicit their advice, but I didn't think about doing the same exact thing with the faceless mob, so to speak, or audience I should say maybe. And the other thing you can ask too, is one of my favorite questions, how much money have you spent trying to solve this problem already? Oh. And if the answer is none, that's a very strong signal. Or if the answer is, I wouldn't even dare count, well, that's a very strong signal too. Because there are audiences out there that are really interested and will have a lot of dialogue with you. And then when you ask, how much money have you spent on this already, and they're like, oh, I don't have, none. And basically, I don't have money to spend on this, but it sure is a big problem. That's a really good piece of information to have. And there are some audiences that just don't spend money on things for whatever, even though they have serious issues. They have things that they, everything else looks like it would indicate that they would be receptive. They just, for whatever reason, don't spend money on it. And thank you for your service, by the way, Mike. All right, let's jump to the next question. All right, so this is a fast, this is actually a really fascinating question. The person who submitted this question wants, eventually, to have a business that's generating $5,000 a month after taxes, and he currently has a $47 product. And that's the only product, it's a front-end product, so it's very much, it's not really a direct response model, it's much more of an e-commerce model, because he just wants to sell this $47 product, and to get to that $5,000 a month after taxes, and he wants to know how to estimate its traffic cost to do that. This is not my favorite answer. My second favorite answer is it depends, or my second least favorite answer is it depends. My first least favorite answer is it's impossible to know. This is an impossible to know question, or I'm sorry, it's impossible to estimate in advance, because we don't know how well the offer converts, we don't know the audience, we don't know how well the offer will convert to the audience, we have none of that information, we don't know what it costs to interact with that audience, or those audiences. So I mentioned this question not to give a not answer, but I mentioned this question, but it's a really important question that we need to understand, that I've seen these charts and infographics and other things to say, the average cost of a lead on Facebook is X. They're absolutely meaningless. If one person's paying $100 lead and the other person's paying a dollar lead, the average cost per lead is 50. Neither one of those people are paying $50 for the lead, and it doesn't separate audiences, it's meaningless. So the only way to answer this question is, in my opinion, to identify an audience that should be most receptive to the offer. This person has to specify who we believe his audience is, and then to allocate some amount of money, 200, 300, 150, whatever it is, allocate that money and run a test to see if that audience buys that product and at what rate. So, and that's just an initial data point, and lots of things can happen. You can say, I want to spend $100 to see if this offer, the way I've presented it, will convert to this audience. You may spend $100 and get zero sales. Now, is it the audience or the offer? In that case, we don't know. We have to dig a little bit deeper and look at the people go from the add to landing page, and then they didn't convert. Well, we have to deconstruct why. Or you might spend $100, you might make 300 in sales. That's a different piece of data. In that case, do more. So it's unfortunate, but when we're running paid traffic, this is the reality and the numbers change over time. They consistently and they change, they're very different per platform and very different per audience. And we get into AdWords this week. In the next two weeks, we'll see that they're, we calculate this at the keyword phrase level. So, I know that's not the answer. I suspect that's not the answer that everybody wants to hear, but what's also critical in this example is CPA, AOV, and LTV are not the way to think about a business like this. This would actually be a ROAS return on ad spend. When you sell a single product and you want to make your profit on that product, then return on ad spend is a better metric. It's ROAS return on ad spend, it's pronounced ROAS. That is a better metric because you're saying, I am spending X amount of dollars and in return I'm getting X amount of dollars. And some ratio usually north of four to one, somewhere between four and eight to one, depending on your industry, those are sort of broad targets for ROAS. So, if you spend $5, you would want to get $20 plus. And how you figure that out is to go out and do the work and to see what result you get and then continually to iterate towards success. That's just the reality of it. All right, next question. This is about LBC and the traffic engine. So, LBC is based on a low cost content for this particular person, this person built on a low cost continuity program. Like the question is, what about a backend? How does that factor in? Again, backend, you do not have to have, this was a comment with the question with Mike, you do not have to have that in place to start. You can use LBC with traffic engine and you can have a very low cost initial offer, very low cost continuity. You're just learning the economics. And that's what's so critical about these early phases is we have to internalize a conceptual understanding of the economics that we need to know that we've put a dollar out onto a traffic platform and we need to know how much comes back from that investment. And when does it come back? And maybe what you learn with LBC is that you put $7 out into the world and it brings back $7 over the first 30 days. Well, excellent. You've just made a free customer generating machine. That's amazing. Free customers, that's the gold standard. So pat yourself on the back. You may put $7 out and you might get $14 in return and that's staggeringly good or you might put $7 out and make $3 back. But the same idea underlies all of this is that when we're spending money for paid traffic, we have to understand the economics of it or we're essentially either wasting money because we're not looking closely enough or we're making assumptions that somehow that money magically will return. And it does to be in some examples, brand advertising, that's the fundamental concept of brand advertising, that you're building awareness by spending money and that awareness comes back. That just doesn't work at the level that we're talking about. That works for Nike, works for Porsche, that doesn't work so well for a smaller business. So to learn those economics, that's the short term. And you can learn that with any methodology. If you've invested in LBC and you're doing that work, use that. That will give you the understanding that you need. And once you have that understanding, then you actually have the power. This is a teach to fish program, not a, I've done the fishing for you and just do this and fill in the blanks. You are going to learn how to fish in this program well beyond whatever example you're using when you go through it the first time. That's the whole point is to, it's this is skill transfer. It is the ability to take what you're learning and apply it to something and then use that something to learn how to apply it broadly and whenever other somethings that you want to apply it to whether it's expanding what you're currently doing or working with clients and doing that or starting a new venture and doing that, whatever it is you are learning how to do the work and how to think about doing the work in a way that's incredibly efficient and effective. That you're not paying attention to 99% of these, of what doesn't matter. You're looking at the 1% or 5% whatever it is that really drives the big results and focusing on that. That's how this entire program is framed. All right, there's a second question here too. So the question was, this is sort of restating something I said. I said, you said that Google's users think in terms of what is, how do I, what's the best way to, could you please be explicit about what questions to keep in mind when writing copy for Facebook? What are the questions in minds of Facebook users when you're looking for entertainment or as isolate information and education? This is a really great question. I'm so glad you asked this because it's critical to understand that Google's users are thinking in terms of questions. Facebook users are not. Don't try to figure out the questions Facebook users are asking because they're not asking questions. They're not searching for something. They are stumbling on what has showed up in their newsfeed, which was, is heavily influenced by what they're already interested in, but there is no question. The question might be, what's about to show up in my slot machine of a, of a newsfeed, but there's, they're not asking questions. You are interrupting them. You are showing up, your advertisement in Facebook is showing up in their personal social space along with pictures of their friends, kids, pictures of their kids, video, funny cat videos, you name it. That's what your ad, that's the context for your ad. They're not looking for it. So this, I can't overstate how critical this distinction is. This is passive potential interest in Facebook. Google is active in 10. We don't browse Google. We don't go to like, hey, what's new on Google today? That's not how it works. But it's, if you're, if you're waiting in line to check out at the grocery store and you pull out your phone and you sort of randomly spin through your newsfeed, that's a very different experience. And you're not going in thinking, wow, I really hope I find a video today about X or I really hope I learned about what, it's not that at all. So just the mindset has to change dramatically. We'll talk about that a lot when we get into Facebook. I will start talking about it this week in module three when we were sort of have orienting our mind how AdWords works. All right, next question. This is actually looks like there, now there's several questions. This person is, has run an agency for 20 years, just to frame the concepts. So first thing he says is what we offer is in three areas, user experience and digital transformation, website development and support, digital marketing and SEO. My thoughts are that I will need to pick one area to concentrate on as the offer. My thoughts are that I will need to pick one area to concentrate on as the offer is quite different. That's not necessarily true. Maybe, let's think about this for a second. So if you, if you have three proven offers, meaning you've sold user experience and digital transformation, website development and support and digital marketing and SEO, then you have proven offers. You've made money selling them. You've been in business for 20 years. So I imagine those are pretty well proven offers. So you could roll out one offer at a time. And I'm guessing it's a different audience for each. If it's the exact same audience, that's a little more interesting because you might wanna go one level higher and have some way to assess which of those places is the best place to start. But the thing to keep in mind, the reason I really like this question for everybody's benefit is, we know that we have multiple offers. The customer, the prospect seeing into our world only sees that what we've shown them. So somebody could be searching, with an in Australia for user experience, XYZ, whatever the search terms would be. And that person would see your offer specific to that need, but might not know initially that you also do website development support and digital marketing and that's fine. They don't need to know that. It's not like you're hiding it from them. It's just not relevant to them at that time. Now maybe later in your sales cycle, that would be beneficial for them to know. But initially we are trying to match the closest, the most relevant audience to the most relevant offer. So it's sort of a perfect audience to the perfect offer as closely as we possibly can, and then build out from there. And you can do that with three offers simultaneously. If you have the budget and budget of time, energy and dollars to support that. Or if you think one of your offers is a home run, it just does better than the others. Or if you get more joy from one offer, whatever it is, you could start with one or start with all three or start with two or you could also put all three out and figure out which one, the audience, if it's the same audience, which one resonates most. Lots of ways to do this, but don't feel like you have to pick one offer and just focus on that. Unless it takes away from your ability to focus on it. Sometimes it's easier to do the same thing across three or four examples or two or three examples. And other times, just because of the overhead of creation, it's harder. So stack the deck in your favor, park in a down L slope, pick one, two, but three. And I would suggest picking the one that converts the best because that'll give you the strongest signal. Second question, same person, my offer is generally a higher ticket price item. I do have lower cost items like audits, marketing plans, et cetera. However, ultimately we want to build a customer's website and then have them transition through the other offers. He says I have a value ladder-ish. I love that. I'm not really sure what my question is here, other than the higher ticket price and quantifying whether my lead-gen tactics are working has been quite challenging or downright annoying, preach, brother preach. So the first thing I'd recommend doing is map a typical customer journey or journeys. Just look back on the last five years, three years. Like how does somebody find you? If it's 100% word of mouth, just do that. Just be sort of honest and upfront with yourself how do I get customers? Like 99% of my business is through word of mouth. The irony is not lost on me, by the way. That my agency business, 100% of my consulting business is word of mouth. If I were to do a customer journey, it's a pretty weird journey. It's kind of like invest 25 years in your career and meet enough people who do the work over a couple, two and a half decades, meet other people who have been doing the work for a similar amount of time, develop long-term relationships based on value and then profit, right? It's kind of an interesting business model that I don't necessarily recommend to everybody. But that's my actual customer journey. If I look back on customers I have, that's the reality. So what I would recommend that you do with this exercise is map your typical customer journeys, meaning how did they hear about you? After they heard about you, what happened next? Did they call? Did they email? Did they read a blog post? Whatever it is. What you're looking for are the inflection points. When did they first hear about you? When did they first interact with you? How, what was the nature of that interaction? What additional interactions did it lead to? And then the big one is what did they first purchase? And after that purchase, what did they purchase next? How did they purchase that next thing? Like what made them aware of it? Like you just need kind of an understanding of it. This isn't decimal place accuracy, but you can do it with a crayon or a piece of paper. But just, and if it's the exact same journey, excellent. My guess is it's not. I guess you have two or three different journeys. And what you need to ask yourself is, within these different journeys, what makes them different? As you'll see it from your perspective that they offer, but what is it about the audience? What distinguishing characteristics of the audience make this value, this customer journey different than this customer journey? Is ultimately what we're, that allows us then to find that particular audience and show them the right first thing, second thing, third thing. What you're trying to do with this is to now work backwards to a starting point that you can use paid traffic to do the first part of that engagement. So if most people hear about you through word of mouth, you have to reverse engineer word of mouth at a larger market. So you need to have some way to make that first contact. That's the equivalent of word of mouth. And then take them to the next thing that somebody who hears about you through word of mouth usually does next or next or whatever is after that. So you just need to map that first part. And then know that becomes your traffic engine. That initial, whatever part of that journey is, happens with paid traffic and then everything else happens either by email or through a direct one on one consultation or the sales team or whatever it is, however your process works for that. All right, let's move on. This is, there's a lot of, this is like 10 questions in one email. All right, my first question is about systems theory. When I read about it in Andre's work and intuitively made a lot of sense, at the same time, it sounds like you're, it sounds like using mostly your gut feeling to know how to optimize your system. I mean, I hardly get enough traffic to run a statistically relevant split test to decide on a headline. If I wanted to test the impact of one element on the entire system, I would need an incredible amount of traffic running through the system. Yes, sure, you use principle number, okay, yes, sure, you use principle number five, focus on the longterm happiness of your customer and a lot of decisions are being taken care of. But I guess that you still would want to know what sales page converts better. You would want to eliminate any pushy, sleazy elements from the sales page to artificially increase sales. But if you have two equally honest sales pages, wouldn't you want to test which one performs better? Okay, so there's a lot there, great, I appreciate these questions, these are great questions. Gut feeling is not how to optimize systems, just to be very, very clear. If I, if I see, if, and how I described it, I suggested that I apologize because that is not at all on how to optimize a system. The gut feeling, which I think where the miscommunication happened is that's when you decide what you're optimizing the system for, right? What is your goal as a business owner or as a creative professional or as a service provider? What are you optimizing your system for? It could be maximum money, that's what, you know, that's a very common one, like how do I get this thing that I'm building to make me the maximum amount of money possible? That's a way to optimize. Sometimes it's quality of life, like, you know, I don't need a ton of money, but I only want to work with people I really like or whatever it is. Sometimes it's some combination where it's, and I need to make a certain amount of money to feel valued, but I, but anything over a certain amount doesn't really add as much utility, so there's a balance, I want a certain amount of money and a certain quality of life. That's the gut feeling part, like, I can't, and when I say gut feeling, it's really, I can't answer that question for you because I have no idea what your criteria is for a successful business, and I shouldn't, there is no, there is no defining answer to that other than your answer. The question, you know, we, in order to know if our business system is producing the outcomes we've identified as being important, we need to know what those outcomes are, and in order to know where the constraint is in that, we need to know what that, what we're optimizing for. That's really where gut feeling comes out. After that, optimization is, systemic optimization is, is the system I've produced creating that result, and if it's not, where, why do I think it's not, like if I look at it holistically, not why aren't the parts, because that's sort of the critical element of systems theory, the parts don't produce the results, the results of the system emerge from the interaction of the parts. So, you know, sort of running down to the level of this sales page versus this sales page, maybe that's the solution, but my guess is it's, maybe higher, like, might be higher level or messaging or something like that. Okay, second question. This is about Facebook, so I'm gonna go through this very quickly. Good point to rely on the big data of Facebook, which means that going for a sales conversion is completely different than asking Facebook for leads, but initially when you don't have enough buyers on your Facebook pixels, don't you have to first optimize for leads? We're going to talk about this in depth when we get to the Facebook section. This has been asked different ways, like different people and comments and other things, so I'm gonna address this very quickly. There are certainly situations where, because of the volume of traffic that we're running, we have to optimize for something other than the end goal that we want. That could be because of timing, like it takes 30 days before we make a sale the way our system is designed, we can't optimize for that, and that's completely possible, or you don't have enough data and you have to optimize for something else. All of that is true, it's an issue we all deal with, but what's critically important to understand is that the way we think about optimizing for leads is different than the way Facebook thinks about optimizing for leads. We see the relationship between opt-in and purchase, because that's how we view our business. We see that in order to make a purchase, somebody has to have opted in. Therefore, there is a causal relationship between purchase and lead. And Facebook looks at opting in the likelihood or the proclivity to opt-in to become a lead as a discrete piece of information that has no causal relationship to becoming a customer. When you tell Facebook that you are optimizing for leads, it will go find people who opt-in for things. Now, is there a relationship between the opt-in and eventual purchase? In your system there is, in Facebook's perspective, there isn't, and they are not explicit about telling you that. So it seems like we're just doing the next best thing, but in reality, we're approximating a next best thing. And you can get really good at optimizing for leads and not generate a single customer. So it's just an awareness at this point. Okay, third question, not really a question, rather a takeaway. The challenge is to keep AOV and LTV over the CPA. I love that you're using these terms, by the way. This is great, thank you. That means that in the medium term, we're always increasing advertising costs. Only the companies that keep adding on new products ordered to increase the LTV will be able to survive. The more products you can sell to your happy customer, well, let's address this first. That's not true. I'm sorry, with always increasing advertising costs, only the companies that keep adding on new products ordered to increase the LTV will be able to survive. That's not true, because you can reduce CPA, especially as CPA gets really off the charts, expensive, you can reduce CPA. It's just there's a floor to it. But I guess the bigger thing is here, I don't want you to have that as a takeaway, that you always have to add new products. It's just, it's not true. So LTV, one of the things that we talked about early on in the manifesto is that there's four times more opportunity after 90 days in revenue opportunity. So right there, we know the economics of an initial offer if you're using a Legion on the front end, that over two years you're going to get four times. If you do it right, you're not a jerk and you interact with your audience positively. You're going to get a significant return over the next two years anyway, based in a, compared to people who has a more short-term focus. So that's part of it, LTV calculation. I just, I don't want you to think about that. This is an offer that the paid traffic necessitates that you're just cranking out offer after offer, product after product. That's not the outcome that I want you to think about. So continuing this, the more products you can sell your happy customers, the higher your long-term value and the more probable you make up for the initial loss you take from the high CPA. But that also means that if you're starting out or presently you're only selling one product, it will be much more difficult to really get to break even. It's almost like you have to buy yourself into the market, making an upfront investment. You hope to recover when you keep adding on more backends, right? No, no, no, no, no, no, no. I have seen, and I've used these examples in emails and comments and other places, and I'll share a couple of them now. I'll share two. So I know someone who spent $700 on Facebook. He generated from that $700 approximately 400 email leads. Of those 400 email leads, wonderful human being really used email to interact with this audience to really understand their needs. He realized the best way to communicate with that audience was not through email, it was with a Facebook group. So he then opened up a Facebook group where he gives lots of content, clothes, private Facebook group, they have the drawing, and interacts with clients in that Facebook group. So here's the initial economics. $700 gets 400, I think it was 421 people on email list. That in turn, there were 200, 250 or so people on in a Facebook group initially. He interacted with that Facebook group over, I think it was two or three months, just leading with value, really understood what the audience wanted put together and offer based on those needs and generated $96,000 in revenue from a $997 munch coaching program. So I'm not suggesting that tomorrow you're going to spend $700 and figure out a way to make $96,000. But there's a very valuable lesson in that, that what was his cost to acquire a customer? Well, initially he had the money to invest. He put $700 into it. So with no understanding of what was going to happen, he just got 400 people. He did a traditional lead magnet. I mean, this was just a test and he got these people and interacted with them and it took a while, but then realized there was incredible value there. So there are lots of ways to come at this once you sort of understand how the pieces fit together. Now, in that same situation, if he had asked me on the front end, what do I do? I would not say just keep running that and continue to spend, I don't know if it took six weeks or so to get to spend the $700. I would not say, we'll just keep spending $125 a week and let's see what happens. The way he did it was perfect. He spent a certain amount of money and he got an audience and then he interacted with that audience. And then he made an offer that that audience had already told them that they wanted through because he was leading with value. So it's a different way to approach it, but it's in its age. And I suspect in the future now, and I think he has since done this, now he knows the economics of that machine a little bit better. So now he can go back and say, okay, let me turn on this machine that generates email leads, which I then turn into Facebook group members, which I then turn into coaching program members because he has proved the efficacy of that model through that initial test. So that's a critical distinction. Don't get round up and I may go back and make some edits to the initial copies. I don't want you to feel like you have to have, I mean, he has a back end there and no front end, right? That's quite a back end. And I know any of us on this call, if you said, hey, would you be willing to trade $700 for $96,000? I think we're all going to enter, yes. Another example that I've seen, I was not involved with this. It's a unicorn example that I use, it's sort of one of the things that I've deconstructed because it was so profound, but it was somebody who, and I'm not entirely sure about the numbers. I have two numbers in my notes, either one of them is staggeringly impressive, so I'll share both of them. This was somebody who knew nothing about Facebook at all, had, was sort of not well known, but well known to a few in a particular space and put together sort of the magnum opus of I believe it was her career to, I'm going to take people through this training. Here's what it's going to be. I know this is what you need. The ads were clunky. It was just, it violated every rule of what should work. And I think that person spent 400 or so, 450, and the two numbers I remember are 42,000 or 76,000 in revenue from it, like very small ad spend. And I've seen that happen a lot. I've seen, I'm going to say a lot, I don't know, 10, 12, 15 times, where, and I think it's because, and I've talked about this before, I think it's because a person so close to the market speaks the language of the market and knows the felt needs so closely that the way they express it is just so powerful. But I've seen the same phenomenon. And it's tough for me as an agency owner, and it's one of the reasons I do less agency work and more consulting and more sort of teaching work is because it's hard as an agency to come in and compete with that. And what often happens, and I've seen this happen several times, and I've been in the unfortunate position of being the person who was responsible for this sometimes, where an owner or the person closest to the work did, they learned some way to do it and they went out and they did something that was incredibly successful initially, they spent $2,000 or $3,000 and they had a $100,000 launch. And then they want to quote unquote do it right, and they hire an agency and everything changes. And then they spend $40,000, $50,000, and they have a $150,000 launch. And what is that? Is it because I'm awful at what I do? I hope not. But I've seen that phenomenon enough, where you go does that mean every small business owner needs to do their own thing? No, but there's something about being really close to the needs of the audience. I think that's the big lever. And the copy doesn't sound like copy. It sounds like a human being talking, and this is what we're gonna do when we get into writing Facebook copy in particular, we're gonna talk about this a lot. Because I have spent a long time trying to reverse engineer the unicorns that I've seen, and I have a pretty good idea what's driving their success. So this is all really around this idea of, don't feel like just because I've given you a set of metrics in some examples that you're going to have that same example. I work in some of the most difficult competitive markets imaginable, so there are often razor thin distinctions here. You might be in a market where you spend a dollar and you make 15 on the front end. And if that's you, that is amazing. I just wanna make sure that everything that I'm talking about in this course applies to that situation, and the more likely situation where you're gonna find yourself in a competitive environment. So don't get hung up on the details, let's keep this conceptual. Sorry. All right, next question. This one might have been, nope, this wasn't that long. Okay. Given different awareness levels of our intended audience, does best practice include creating multiple ads with copy that talks to each level? I wanna be cautious here, is now we're jumping ahead to writing ads and we should not be writing ads right now. Continuing, as in someone may be searching ways to lose weight and okay, so someone may be searching ways to lose weight and another may be searching set up variations. His program would help them get the results thereafter, but the two conversations would be totally different. With the activity that makes the biggest impact be to write ads for the latter audience, the ones that already believe working on is the answer, or the former, once you know that they need to lose their beer bellies, but don't know where to start. On one end, I see the value of targeting a problem aware audience because you can potentially stand out as the first person to introduce a new and exciting solution. On the other end, I also see value in targeting a solution aware audience because their beliefs are likely closely aligned to your own. The initial answer here is both, right? They do both, but do them separately because you're speaking to the underlying principles we wanna meet every single prospect where they are. So solution aware versus problem aware, sort of the overall awareness scale, there's unaware. They have no idea there's even a problem or a potential solution to a problem they don't know they're not even aware of. There's problem aware, which is, I can't see my toes anymore, but I don't know what to do about it. And then there's solution aware, which is I can't see my toes anywhere anymore. I sort of look through a lot of options available to me and I think exercise is the best one. And I know here's an option around exercise. Those are very different conversations. What I would recommend is you're not going to know until you know, which means you're not going to know the answer to these questions until you have a well-structured experiment to answer those questions. So that would most likely, and I think really the only way to do this is through search, you might be able to sort of approximate it. Actually let me talk out loud for a second. A few ways to do this. One, search is an easy way to find problem aware versus solution aware because they use that in their language. And in general, you'll find that the solution aware people are more likely to convert and depending on the economics of the cost of the click, usually more likely to be ROI positive, usually. That's maybe not true for you. Facebook, I think it would be harder to find these people on Facebook. So I might do one of two things. And not when I say harder to find, I mean harder to distinguish because they're not looking. And I don't know what factors that would show up in somebody's interest that would indicate that they're problem versus solution aware. So I think on Facebook, I would, here's what I think I would do and then I'll tell you what I might also do. What I think I might do is have two different ads speaking to the two different audiences. One would be speaking to the people who can't see their toes, realize they've packed onto pounds, know there's a problem, don't even know where to start. And then I would approach them from that point and sort of take them through the big levers they could pull to change that situation and eventually lead them to, I would eventually lead them to my solution and that would take a little bit longer. The solution aware people, I would have a different conversation with, I would sort of acknowledge right up front. I might even say, you have this issue and you know this is really the best way to deal with it and let's talk about it from there and sort of go forward from there. Now, I don't think, so that's what, that's sort of initially what my gut tells me I would do because I would wanna know what, which one of those messages resonated the most with people or is there value in having both of those messages out in the same audience simultaneously because I can't really distinguish whose solution awareness, problem aware on Facebook. I also think I might put all of that into a single ad and taken through the entire journey and I might rate the ad so it does both and I would probably test that ad against the other two individually because I would really want to know, I wanna know if I could do it all in one ad maybe. I don't know if there's a huge value in that. I'm just, again, a lot of what I'm gonna do with you on these Q and A calls is just think out loud so you see how I think and more importantly that you realize that there is the, what you see, it's funny, what you see with a module that's written is very precise because of the precision of language and how important that precision is to me. I also want you to be very clear that I don't sit down and write those modules, start to finish the way you read them. It is much more of a dialogue that I work through over and over and over again till I get there. So the Q and A calls are opportunities for you to sort of hear that crazy dialogue I have with myself and how I do think out loud about these things. There is no right answer. There's directionally right answers, there are probabilistically more likely answers, but the question that I would really have in your scenario to get back to your fundamental question is, you have to treat them differently to test to get an answer. So write an ad that's specific to problem aware, write an ad that's specific to solution aware, write another ad that combines both, it's longer, it sort of takes them on a journey and then wherever you have met them in the ad where you take them next has to be the next logical step in that. So if somebody's problem aware, you can't just take them to, you can't sort of, oh okay, here's your problem, here's a solution, here's the sales page for the solution, then if somebody's solution aware, here's your solution, go click here and buy it. You need to meet the problem aware people. It's a longer journey, you're educating before you're selling much longer. You're still educating before you're selling with problem aware, but the education is around the solution versus also explaining that there's sort of the lots of different options for solutions and this is the one that's the best in your opinion. For example, selling an online course and adding coaches, calls to it, having them sell for the coaching, et cetera. Short answer is no, I'm not going to, I can't define your price point for you, and I shouldn't. I do want to address something, though. I mean, you'll kind of get there, and price is a funny thing. Like when I teach at Vermont Tech, one of the questions I ask students is we have to define value, and it's like an hour long conversation, and we get to this point where we realize we don't actually know what value is, and the value, the definition of value that I sort of reveal, that I found, I don't know where I found this, I didn't invent it. As value is the difference between what you pay for something and what you get for it. So if I go to Starbucks and pay $2.60 for a small cup of coffee, the value of that cup of coffee to me is greater than having $2.60 in my wallet. For some people, several years ago, I think it was the most expensive forward-mustaining ever sold, so it won over a million dollars at auction. Was it worth it? You see this question a lot, was it worth it? Well, it was clearly worth it because it sold for over a million dollars to somebody out in the world having that million dollar Mustang in his or her collection was worth more than having the million dollars. That's what value is. When we talk about pricing, pricing just all comes down to, it comes down to a lot of factors. How are you trying to have more students or fewer students? Lots of different things going to it. We'll talk about that probably on the next, I'm talking myself in the answer in the question, which is funny. We'll probably talk about that a little bit on the next Q&A call. And one thing I do want to address in this question is the quote, and it's in quotes, packaging of the offer. Packaging of the offer is the offer, right? That's what I want to really emphasize that, that what goes into the offer is the offer. The online course is not the offer. So the online course is the product. The online course with a live Q&A, with this at a discounted price is an offer. That packaging is the offer. That's distinct, this is such, it took me a long time to understand this distinction. I really want you all to understand this distinction, that it is all of the things that are in exchange for the sale and that moment in time are what constitute the offer. That it is not the underlying product itself. And that knowing that difference is critical. All right, next question. Will we go into how to define front end versus back end offers? Yeah, I mean, yeah, we kind of have to. If so, how do we define a front end offer that delivers awesome value without cannibalizing back end offer? We'll talk about that. How to find a sweet spot between too little value and too much. I don't know necessarily that you can give too much value on the front end. I mean, I get it, I get the question here, right? Like the question is, if we have, I don't know, 100% of a certain amount of content, and the front end is $97 and we give 80% of that content. And I get that, that's like really the question. But the question, I would sort of change the question and go upstream a little bit and say, I have this opportunity, I've made the boundaries around what the front end is, right? It's going to be about this thing, but there is no boundary about how much I'm going to give you a value about that thing. I'm just not going to give you lots of other value about these other things that are in the back end that you need to look at later. Like that's how I look at it. Like I don't, I never want to be in a position where I'm thinking on the front end, okay, I can only tell you 10% of what you need to know about this topic because I'm charging you 10% of what the core offer, I hate that idea. What I want to say instead is I have this thing on the front end that I think is really the first thing you might be interested in. And I am going to give you my heart and soul about that first thing over the top because I want you to understand it backwards and forwards. And then after that, the next thing that I think will be valuable to you, I'm going to do the same thing with that. It's much more expanded and everything else, but I'm not trying to mix them together, if that makes sense. That may not make sense to anybody. Anything to add on that, Andre? Or am I, Lauren? I think that's a great way to frame it for people. Yeah, Lauren just threw something in the chat too. Thank you, Lauren. He said, you can't give too much value on your front end, but don't confuse tons of content with value. It's better for people to actually consume your front end content. And just for everybody's benefit, I mentioned this every time Lauren and I have a conversation so everybody knows. Lauren and I have worked together for a year and a half or so. So Lauren's point is very well taken here that they're confusing like, oh, I have 12 bonuses and you get 86 videos. I mean, how many times have you purchased something and felt like, oh my God, I don't need these 300 things, right? I don't need the 80 expressions of this one thing. And in fact, there's often much greater value in cutting through all that bullshit and saying, instead of giving you 1,000 things to think about, knowing that you'll be tired by a number 200 and you'll never get through them, instead of the 1,000, I'm gonna tell you the three that matter most and how to get the most value out of knowing these three things in the next 72 hours. That's valuable. That's what sells the next thing. The person who can cut through all the clutter and say, I could tell you 1,000 things only of which six actually would benefit you, but instead I'm going to tell you those six and I'm gonna tell you right now today how to get the most out of one of them. That is extreme value. Those are the people you wanna buy more from. Assuming your industry or your thing does, sort of is amenable to that, which most are. You know, for time out, e-commerce, you know, you can't buy a lot of product stuff that's obviously not relevant to that. Later, I will do that. We get to that point. Quick jump ahead. Let's see. Next one quote, few things matter. How do you determine what elements are part of the 1%? How do you determine the impacts? Assuming this one will be bringing an answer in the vein of hypothesis to be tested. I'd be interested to know what would be the basis for the hypothesis? How do you measure the results of the test? So the hard question. So yes, and we know no one quotes. We know that in general, few things matter. And figuring out what those few things are, it's hard work and it's worth the effort. So it's also, we have to be cautious. I had this conversation with your friend the other day. So like, he's talking about, my question was to this person who knows a lot about the 80, 20 principle was, is it true? Do we really think the 80, 20 principle that is really derived from large data set thinking applies at the individual level? So this thing that Tim Ferriss did for the four hour work week, and you hear this a lot. Like, how many people have you heard say, I did an 80, 20 analysis on my time, on my business and it changed everything. I don't know, and I can find no evidence that suggests the 80, 20 principle scales down to the level of the individual. It's a concept. Maybe it's true. I don't know, but it is not at all what Pareto was talking about. He was talking about large data sets. So this idea that few things matter, this conversation that I had with a friend the other day was his counter argument was, well, I just worked on this webinar and that webinar is probably going to generate tens of thousands of dollars in sales. So isn't it true that the hour and a half that I spent working on this webinar is worth way more money from the perspective of his use of his time than the other things he did that day? And the obvious answer is yes, there are things that are more important, but the less obvious answer is that webinar exists within a broader context that if that broader context didn't exist, the webinar would be useless. Like he can't just go out and look, hey, I have this webinar and it generates money. It doesn't do what it's on its own, it's part of the system. So my, this is a very long, sort of queerness route to get back to this question. How do we find the 1%? I think that's, we've got to be really, really careful when we do this exercise that we don't get reductionist and say, well, I have 10 things that happen in my business. And I think because this thing is the closest to the point at which the money starts to show up, that must be more important to let me focus there. I think that's a dangerous assumption. I think what we need to do instead is to look a little more broadly and realize that there are things that by their nature are more important. Understanding your audience, critically important. Knowing the language of your audience, critically important. Taking the time to understand the felt needs of your audience, that thing that really motivates them. And that's part two of the audience in the Offer Masterclass is where we're going next. Those are really high value activities. And when I see a lot, and this is sort of one of my pet peeves, maybe more than this is one of several pet peeves, but this is a big one, which is, we see something that's successful, like a big promotion that's successful, and everybody sits down and dissects that promotion. What did that thing do? And why did it do that? And they look at the structure of it, it's like, oh, well, there were three upsells instead of one, they look at the parts. But the reality was the thing that drove everything was that the idea was really valuable. It was a really interesting insight that somebody had that they expressed well through the written word. And everything that follows was the byproduct of that. Like the whole structure seemed better, but only because the messaging and the idea was so good. So if you have a, and this is something I say to clients a lot when they ask me about what should I expect. And the thing I say early on is, if you have a really good offer, I can look like a hero. And if your offer is terrible, there's probably unlikely that I can do anything. I have a client that's in the ski resort primarily in the wintertime. And when they hired, I do the digital marketing, when they hired a marketing agency, the owner of the marketing agency took me to lunch and said, what do I need to know about this client and this market? And I said, it's simple. When it snows, we're heroes. And when it doesn't, we're idiots. And she looked at me and said, it can't be that simple. I said, it is that simple. That it is that simple. So these ones, getting back to this few things matter, don't gloss over the big things like the value proposition and we're gonna get into in part two of the audience and offer masterclass. Don't gloss over the fact that the thing that you're selling, the impact it makes on the person who buys it is a thousand times more important than how you sell the damn thing. How you sell it is so, and I understand that you're being taught something different. And I don't wanna name any names about, I mean, I will, but right. Like if you go to funnel hacking live, the underlying message of click funnels and Russell Brunson, who I don't know personally and this is not a dig at him personally, but the underlying message is if you just have the right template to follow and the right technology to execute that success follows. And he's partly right. You need the ability to bring your offer out into the world, but if your offer is not valuable, no amount of technology or structure or wishful thinking is going to make it work. The offer has to be excellent for the person who's paying the money for it. It has to be far more valuable for them to have it and to have the money that they're exchanging. That's a big mover. That's a few things matter. Don't ever forget that you're in the business of changing somebody's life in exchange for some value. Maybe a little bit, maybe you're solving the problem that's a nagging issue or maybe that you're showing them away that's going to change their life for the better for the next 50 years, but you're changing lives with what you're selling and how that change happens and what the change is and who it matters to, that's the big driver. The rest of the mechanisms to get there, that's gone. Andre, you must have some thoughts about this. I'm sick of hearing my own voice. Yeah, let me add another layer to this or another perspective anyway. And I guess you've expressed an element of this inside of your part one of your offer, your audience and offer masterclass. That is the few things that matter. That's subjectively, for me, it's going to be subjectively different than it is for you. So for example, there's things that I just can't or won't do because I don't like it, I don't enjoy it, I refuse to do it, which is video and audio, kind of like what I'm doing now. And I've chosen to focus on the written word, right? So all of our marketing is around that, is around long form copy and that's never gonna change. When it does finally change, I'll go and write books. But until this is the way that I express stuff and within the context of that, that's gonna be one of the few things that matter for me. So I have to go and get a writing coach because it's the one thing that I have to focus on because I don't want to or don't have the ability to do any of the other things. Now for other people, they might be great with video and terrible at the written word. Well, then there are a few things that matter, it's probably gonna fit into that. Trying to think, we'll zoom out and should I do written or should I do video or should I do this mechanism or that mechanism? And then you're trying to cherry pick the ones that other people are good at and then you're gonna correct your version. Okay, now if I just get good of this, this, this and this, I'm gonna have the world's best thing, system machine. And I don't think that's correct because objectively there is nothing that's the best at whatever. So you need to subjectively figure out what you choose to do, just like what skills you wanna express into the world and the market that you go after. Yes, and I'm kind of rambling now but I think those things that matter once you've figured them out and then you decide to double down and then double down on them, it makes a lot of things easier. Yeah, and I think where we, the other side of that is we will at some point have to start looking for the constraint in our, the systems that we design. And that's hard. The theory of constraints tells us, it's sort of predicated on systems theory and tells us that any complicated system has one and only one constraint. And there's one bottleneck in the whole system. Generally in a small business, the bottleneck is us, it's easy to find. They're also, you know, then you can kind of go meta their belief constraints. You know, for the longest time I had a belief constraint in my business that I didn't see. I sort of saw it all at once as I was getting a cup of coffee and it was sort of this overwhelming, you know, borderline setory moment of like, how did I not see this this whole time? And just to put that in perspective, now I feel like I've opened a loop for everybody, which is fun. I like to do it to Andre, but not everybody. You know, the belief constraint that I had in my business, the question I was trying to solve, and I spent a lot of time on this was increasing revenue with a small business and realizing, I put myself in a choice either I had to have, I had to increase my hourly rate, this is when I was primarily a freelancer with a small team, I had to increase my hourly rate or add to the team of more people who could, who could contribute to the current hourly rate. Those were sort of these two choices that I was wrestling with. And as I was getting my cup of coffee, I realized that if I moved upstream and disconnected my earnings from the amount of time that I worked, then that would solve that problem immediately. And it was how I thought about it was the problem. So belief constraints are sort of tricky. They're hard to find, but you find they're often very powerful. So there is a way to find these things that critically matter functionally, but the things that really matter are not the functional things, like how to do the things, the things that really matter are the expression of what it is that you're doing and the value and how that's valuable to. And I think probably the single greatest, like the highest point of leverage, if I look across two plus decades of clients and tremendous amount of experience, the biggest hinge that's, or the hinge that swings the biggest door by far is connecting what it is that you're doing with the deep internal felt need of the audience. It's not the thing they tell you, right? Like the thing, here's a distinction. If you're in the biz-op space in a way, business opportunity space or make money in online space, when you ask people what they want, they tell you that they want to make money. That's not what they want. That's an expression of what they want. They want to feel capable, they want to be respected. They don't wanna wake up in the middle of the night wondering if they are a failure, like these deep felt needs, when you speak to those needs, either overtly or implicitly, that swings everything. So spending time on that, and really, and that's really the second part of the audience and off of masterclass is really around how we find those things. But that, spend the time there, and everything that follows is more valuable. All right, let's see. Next questioner. And it can be a real person who had a real result. We have to be very careful about making the claim that the thing that we sold got them this result, but we're telling somebody else's story of an experience that they had, that we hope is the same experience that the audience that we've targeted hopes to have. We hope that there, some percentage of that audience, is dealing with something where the solution we have to offer is right for them and that when they see that somebody else had the same things going on that they had that and got a better result, that's attractive to them because this is a social selling environment, capital S social, tiny, tiny, tiny, tiny lowercase S selling. Facebook's not a selling environment. And we're trying to make Facebook do something it was not designed to do. Once you can, this is a quote from me, it's always fun when people quote back to you, ask you what the hell you meant. Once you can literally, this is what I said in one of the modules. Once you can literally see how your business functions structurally and associate the appropriate metrics to each part, everything becomes easier and more effective. The question is, what do you mean by structure? You define it a bit more. Great question. I think structurally, visually, I think structurally. So imagine the simple, let's imagine a four page, multi-page presale site to a seven part email auto responder sequence, a product sequence to an offer page to a order form to a receipt. Like that would be it. And we're going to advertise this through Facebook. So structurally, if I were to draw this, I would put a box for the ad or several boxes for the ads. And then I would have an arrow that went to page one of the multi-page presale site. And I would have each of those pages as individual boxes and arrows to each as a sequential. And then on page four, I would maybe have a little choice, you know, opt in, like a little, a little diamond shape that's sort of the choice. Opt in yes or no. You know, and if no, what happens? And if yes, then they get the first email. And then on the second day of the second email, I'd have all seven emails drawn, just little boxes. And then from the last email, I would have an arrow to another box that represented the sales page, another box that represented the order form and then another box that represented success. I would want to see all of that because now when I see that, I can start putting in numbers. And I can know how that's, let's just for the sake of it, and say that's not all system. The boundaries of the system are on either side of that. So when I sit down and say, well, this ad has a 6% click through rate, I can put a number on it, okay, add 6% of the people who see this ad, click to this page, okay, got it. Or my page one to page two conversion is 15%. People, half the people who see the first page make it to the second page. My first page, the fourth page conversion rate is 10%. So 100 people see page one, 10 of them see page four. My opt-in rate, like I can calculate all of those things because I have drawn out the parts. And because I can see the parts, I can then figure out how to measure the parts in like what measurements matter and what measurements I might want to think about. Because I might want to know, I might want to ask questions like, for every 100 people who come in, who click on an ad and see page one, how many of them are actually become, how many of them see the order page versus how many of them order, right? And I might find out that, let's just say that for every 100 people who come in, 10 see the order page and only one orders. Well, that's interesting. Why would somebody go through four pages of content, seven days of email and only 10% of them finally accept the offer? Where did I go wrong? Because I can see it all, I can start to ask some really good questions. So that's what I need to know about structure of it. It's the boxes and arrows. That would be a simple answer. An individual who is more likely to become a lead according to one indicator, maybe no more, this is me saying this, maybe no more likely to buy online than another user according to another indicator, Facebook's algorithm can do the heavy lifting, it's our responsibility to give a clear instructions on whether it's gonna work. We'll discuss that in modules five and six. How do I opt to listen to this as the question? How do I optimize for sales if I'm running a lead campaign? And a sale might be generated six weeks down the line or I need to wait until, or do I need to wait until modules five and six? Yes, but I did ask for some pointers. So this is a big challenge. There's some comments, Lauren and I have talked about this a little bit in the comments and about different modules. This is a challenge because we want, in an ideal world, we want Facebook to go find the people who do the thing we care about most, in this case, buy. But if they don't buy for six weeks, if they don't buy for 15 days, who knows? That's hard. So the way we break things apart occasionally is we might say the wrong way to do it is say, let's just optimize for leads and know that email do the heavy lifting. That's only true. This is where systems theory is very helpful because if you make the decision to optimize for leads and then focus myopically on things that get you more leads, you're missing the point. So yes, you can optimize for leads, but you can optimize for leads with a system that you built to produce the customers who will eventually buy. So I realize that sounds crazy, but here's the distinction. If we say to Facebook, we want to optimize for leads and we have a multi-page presale site with three pages of content that establishes the beliefs our audience needs to accept and internalize before they can become customers. That's congruent with the overall idea of what we're doing, assuming that's what our final looks like. That makes sense, but if that produces $16 leads and your buddy says $16 leads, I get $2 leads all day long and then you go look at what your buddy's doing, your buddy is offering six free reports and a co-redge landing page with 16 bells and whistles and free videos and you're like, oh, I should do that. You've missed the point, right? Because yes, you get $2 leads and they're worthless generally or they're worth way less than your $16 leads. So what we need to do here is we need to say, sure, we may have to optimize for leads, but we're optimizing for leads within the context of what we expect those leads to do later. We're not just looking at lead generation as a separate thing and trying to do it optimally to get a lower cost per lead. I use that example in one of the modules, I believe, and it's so common, so incredibly common that some of the boxes is like, your lead costs are way too high. It's like, you have to look at it within the context of the system and I am much more willing to pay two, three, four, five times as much for a lead that has gone through lots of hoops because it shows the intent and the quality is much higher than to optimize a system to produce a false positive. So that's really the distinction. Anything contribute to that, Andre? Someone wants to hear your voice in the chat here, I see. A deep, resonant, very white voice. No, I think that's, you've made that point crystal clear and I think that's a great distinction is to build the system as it needs to exist to create the customers you want to create at the end of the day. You don't have a separate system for leads and a separate system for sales. It's one, right? The fact that you can't generate customers initially because it just takes a little while for that to happen, that's fine, but like you said, don't change the system, don't build something different. Yeah. All right, last, this is the last of the written questions that were sent over in advance. Principal number three, focus your time, energy and dollars disproportionately on what matters and performs best. This is a question about my opinion on creating content for organic posts on Facebook. This person says he seems to spend way too much time creating beautiful, so he's told high value content, gets good engagement from this point of view. But when he checks the stats, I see that although a good number of folks visit his website and engage with content, very few appeared opt in to his list or purchase his products. This is really, really critical to understand. I had a client that did this years ago and it was fascinating to me. They spent a quarter of a million dollars on focus groups. This was an e-commerce product. They spent a quarter of a million dollars on focus groups to find their absolutely ideal audience. That audience later when they sort of structured the site or for them and the product and everything else. That audience later was part of a big matrix we created using Facebook advertising to see really where their audience was. Their focus group audience was in there. So we had that and then we had, I think, it was the largest group of variables we ever used. It was crazy. We had 478 ads, which is ridiculous to think about when we first launched this. But what we were trying to figure out is how valuable was that audience to them? Because all I heard about, every time we had meetings there over and over, this is our audience. This is what matters. And again, quarter of a million dollars on focus groups. This is real money. That audience consumed their content at a rate 10 times anybody else. This wasn't a political bent, but there was sort of an interest bent for this particular product. There's a sort of appeal to a type of person. So their core audience that they had spent their money to identify consumed their content. It rates greater than anybody else. They emailed, they commented, they did everything. It rates higher than everything else, except they didn't buy. And when I say they didn't buy, I mean, they did not buy at all. So they optimized, and not because the product didn't sell, the product sold very well, but it sold to entirely different people than they imagined. So, yet I would still have conversations and that still in their mind was their audience. So they would say, but, in looking through this description that this person shared here, like when you get good, you get good engagement, you get all these other things, if that's the business that you're in, that's a great result, right? But if you're not in that business, if you're in the customer business, it's not a great result, it's an irrelevant result. Somebody engages with your content and they do everything that you want them to do, except the thing that matters most that they do, they didn't do anything that you want them to do, right? At the end of the day, they didn't, it gets back to the, and I don't say this to be a jerk, it's just, it's so hard to understand it because it seems like this is exactly what I want to have happen, yet the thing that matters most doesn't happen. If the thing that doesn't matter, most doesn't happen and it doesn't really matter. And it's hard because everything else points the way. Everything's like, well, they're doing all these other things. And that particular client I referenced before, they never abandoned that belief that that was their audience. Their audience, they thought was a particular gender and a particular age and a particular blend of psychographic characteristics. And their actual customer was, for the most part, a different gender in a wildly different location and a different age. Like, I mean, it couldn't have been more, there were like three or four factors that were completely the opposite of who their actual customer was, yet that entire business continued to focus on their quote unquote core customer who never bought. So it sounds weird when I say, like what business would do that? Lots of businesses do that, it's very common. So, I want to be cautious I'm not an organic Facebook guy, that's not my thing. So, I don't know. Let's jump on this first one. Yeah, and we can just, we'll jump on a few, and then just when you jump into, sickly voice. So first question, are we going to test sales pages? How do we have a special method to know if the sales page works? Sort of, I mean, sales page testing to me is less, I mean, I don't, sales page testing to me feels incredibly reductionist, right, unless it's a wildly different sales page. I'd rather test like sales methods. Like when I do my offer, I'm going to do totally different approaches.
