Now, eventually should we test sales pages when we get to a level of refinement later? Yes. But that's more like incremental optimization than I want like exponential differences. So at least in the first eight weeks, we're going to be focusing on the potential for exponential gain. And then once we get the exponential, we'll do incremental later. Let's see, so answered that. Oh, sorry, I can't figure out how this works. Next question, what happens if we cannot spend thousands of dollars and we just start testing with hundreds, spending too much, be a big mistake to avoid is 100 to 500 a good range. And you don't have to spend a fortune, I'll be clear about that. You need to spend enough money to get an answer to the question that you're asking. And if you ask a really good question, you can get an answer faster. You know, you can ask the question, which of these three ads is better? But that's a, that might be a more expensive question to answer because it's three variables. And who knows, lots of different things. But the, you can ask a question, like does this message resonate with the audience that I've targeted and get people to opt in for XYZ? Right, that's a pretty inexpensive test. And we'll talk about sort of how we structure that. You don't need a fortune. You shouldn't start with a fortune to be clear. Starting with a lot of ad spend actually makes this way more difficult. It's much harder to spend the ad spend intelligently when there's a lot of it to kind of keep your eye on the ball and a lot of what we're going to be doing. And really it's the foundation of the success I've had in AdWords is we are going to be so focused on what we're doing that we're going to understand it so well that it's going to be part of our DNA so that we, as we add to it, our understanding grows instead of our confusion grows. It's very easy to add on and have our confusion levels either going incrementally or exponentially to the point where you don't know what's working. And we're going to make things prove that they work or they don't stick around. That's the big difference. Would you say that even if somebody has a relatively big budget that it's better to artificially create a constraint that forces you to create some limitation in how much you can spend daily or weekly on ads? Maybe say, okay, 500 and what can you do with $500 or whatever that number is, but it's way less than the money that you've got in the Macagant. Yeah, that is a really good question. I'll just give you a little bit of a overview. It's that and one other thing. So that by itself is required. It's sufficient, it's necessary but not sufficient. So that's one of two things that people need to do. One, you need to artificially lower your budget. And the other thing you need to know is a different way to use a lower budget intelligently. Because if you just artificially lower your budget, then you're going to get the same pathetic result as less money and frustrating. So I'll use an example that I think will help everybody understand this. I may have hinted at this in the past or I may have even used it, but I think I'm going to use it again in the AdWords module because it's just so powerful. So a former employee of mine worked for a company that did these aftermarket auto parts. And the owner of the company said, here's your budget. And there were two or three different, I think two or three different product line, like different vehicle types within the company. So it was like, you all have to split the budget. And then there were lots of different parts that the aftermarket community wanted. So what ended up happening, and by the time I was called in to look at it, I was called in, they just said, yeah, AdWords doesn't work. And the thing I often say, which doesn't endear me to everybody is the way you're doing AdWords doesn't work. But what they were doing is they were taking this chunk of, it wasn't a ton of money, but it was a chunk of money and they were spreading it out horizontally in a way that it was never going to produce the result that they wanted. It's just impossible. So they could have taken that dollar amount and made it 10% and they still would have had a pathetic result. But they needed to do instead, and this is the exercise that I took them through. They said, okay, where do you ship the products from? And they sort of told me where the location was. I'm like, okay, let's look at the UPS map. Where do you ship the products that they get there automatically in two days by UPS? And they sort of share it on the map, great. So we're going to own, the first decision we're going to make is we're only going to advertise to that area because we're going to put in our ads free second day shipping upgrade, right? They don't have to upgrade because they get it in two days anyway. So we're just going to narrow the geography down, first step. Then I asked, what are the top five products in each of the three different divisions? And they kind of came back with some of the data. These five products overall so better than everything else. Great. What's the number one product in each of those categories for the three? Great. Now we have three products and then we look to the search terms, they're like two or three search terms for each. So then we completely reduced their budget down so that they only sold to people who got the packages in two days, which was free, but they could be presented as an upgrade that was free. And they only sold the number one product for three different categories. And instantly their ROI, their return on ad spend was it was not astronomical, but it was exponential. It was like, I think 17X or something because we did two things. We narrowed their focus dramatically only on like using geography and product and a few other factors and very narrow focus. And we forced them to use a much lower budget. And then what happened after that? Because the question should be, how much can we spend in question should never be asked. It should be as much money as you can get your hands on if it's profitable. So the whole exercise then became, okay, what if we add the second most popular product in that same area? Is it still profitable? Yes, okay, great. What if we add the top five? At what point does the product stop being as profitable as we want? Okay, stop, great. Now go back to the number one product and sell it to the next closest, whatever, five states, but without the free upgrade and shipping. Does that really matter? And in fact, it did really matter. So the economics change, right? But if they hadn't broken the whole thing up that way, they never would have seen how wildly different the economics were by product type and geography and messaging, like they had to do that. So that's the kind of thinking we're going to bring to AdWords. That's what makes AdWords magical. AdWords is, everything in AdWords is context and the context is not obvious. The context is what was the search term somebody made? That's the obvious when people think about, but what time of day did they make it? Where were they when they made it? Like all of these factors, when you turn them just right, you can create a vein of profitability where that profitability is not obvious if you don't turn the dials just the right way. So I said I wasn't even talking about Facebook now, I'm talking about AdWords, we haven't done anything without it. So we'll see. All right, next question. What's the sweet spot for a front-end offer? You have assets ranging from seven to 197. Where do you start? That's a good question. So where do you start? Really, you have to ask yourself how expensive you think the traffic is going to be. I mean, if you can get, you can make $7. Well, that's not the only consideration. Do you wanna build a business on $7 customers? That's a question to ask yourself first. Like that's a different business. And I think Book Castillo talked about this in the podcast she did with Claire Spells, I think is her name. Like they have, I think she's the one who talked about this, maybe not, maybe I'm misremembering it. I've heard someone say this really well somewhere else and we'll get credit, we're credits do. Building a business on $7 customers is a very different than building a business on $197 customers. It's a different customer. They're not bad people, good people. It's no judgment about that at all. It's a different price point and it's wildly different price point. So if it were me, and it's also a lot easier by traffic when you're making $197 on a sale. So if it were me, Gareth, what I would do is I would start with a $197 offer and see if I can sell that profitably. You just have a lot of leeway. Gonna do more work, it's a little bit harder. But a $197 customer, I think you'll be happier with that customer. And $197 is a fine price point for the front end. You might wanna test price points, test $197 versus $147 versus $199 or whatever. I actually don't do $197 versus $199, that's stupid. Do $197 versus $147 versus $97. But that's more of a customer that you want. I've worked with too many clients who got wrapped into the book funnel idea and they generated thousands of $10 customers. And to the person, that wasn't the business they wanted. Now, others to, I know Lauren's thinking of a specific example. Others have turned $10 book sales into upsells into $2,000, I think $2,000 products in a way that was very congruent. And that's really what the free plus shipping book offer was supposed to do. So, but realize when you're doing a free plus shipping price point, $7, $10, $19, that you're getting a customer who wants to spend $7, $10, $20, that's a different customer. And often, another thing to think about too, is while we're thinking out loud, often the $7 sale is not to sell a $7 product, it's to sell a $200 upsell. So, like if you've ever seen any Electroletts ads, it's borderline comical because it's like a $10 sale. And the amount of credibility and proof he puts into selling a $10 product is it's shocking. And when you look at it, like why would he do this? Because he's not selling you the $10 product, he's selling you the upsell. So that's something to be very, to really think about here too, is do you want to lead with $7 or $47 and then upsell the $197 and then all of your content and your, everything is designed to sell the $197? I mean, if you're going to do that, my thought is anyway, just sell the damn thing for $197 and be upfront about it. But maybe not, you know? Here's what I would do now that I've thought about it out loud for everybody to see how insane I am. I would try to sell it at $197. If I can sell it at $197, I would pat myself in the back and tell my friends how smart I am. And be really psyched about that. It'd break even unless you need to make money on it. If for some reason I was struggling, I would sell something less like $27 to $47 and then I would upsell the $197 and see if I can make those economics work. All right, Andre, what would you do? I'm just going to throw this out then that you're going to find that there's certain people that will not buy a $7 offer. They know what's going to come. There's a perception of value. There's all these things floating around in someone's head. And I guess in many ways, I'm looking at this through my lens. I won't buy anything that's a $7 product, but I'll happily spend $500 buying a product. And let's just say for argument's sake, within the same category, because the one is very upfront. I know what I'm going to get. The value proposition is super clear. I can then make it an educated guess that this is going to deliver the value that I'm willing to pay for, but $7 doesn't tell me anything. I'm not going to spend the money on the $7. So I say that only to demonstrate that perhaps by having the $7 offer, you might be losing customers that would have happily paid the $197 and now they're just gone. So you've lost them. They felt like they got the wrong customers. So that's where it came from. Okay, this is a great question. All the questions are great, but when I say great questions, it's one that I kind of want to answer. That's what I mean. But what if you love an audience, like your values align, but you are unsure if they would ever spend money on you? In other words, what if the income of that audience, they can't afford you? Do you change your audience altogether or do you design a product that they can afford? There's no right answer to this. So the purely business person in me says, and I've said this in one of the modules already, that if you are not making money on your business, it's not a business, it's an expensive hobby. And if you want to go have an expensive hobby, I'm not going to tell you not to do that. If this is a social good endeavor and you're willing to spend money to do a social good, and create products that are either inexpensive, absolutely if you're willing to do that. From a business lens, and when I say business, I don't mean transactional. Like I mean just that you're able to feel good about the work that you're doing, that you're compensated well for it, that the value you're bringing into the world, you're getting value in exchange. That's what I mean when I think about business. From that perspective, I would not try to figure out how to extract less money from people who really can't afford it. I would rather suggest that you go find a market where you can make a boatload of money in it, and then, and make so much money in that other market that you can give away the value you could offer to another audience for free. I just, I think there's more satisfying to do that. Because it's just, it's hard. It's hard to take money from people who, maybe they shouldn't be spending, even if the value is a thousand times that, it's just hard. So I would try to find an engine that I don't know who used this phrase, but I love it. Which is that businesses are vehicles for wealth. I would go try to find a different vehicle for that wealth where you can use the same skills that you're developing to make whatever you need to make to feel comfortable, and then take a percentage of your time to do this other thing where your values and audience line up as really more of a hobby, but as a hobby that you can afford because you've done something else. That's how I would approach it. Okay, this is a hard question. Oh, an anonymous attendee. Someone didn't want to be known when they asked this question. I like this. So examples I've used a couple of times in the things I've written, one is GoRuck. I became a GoRuck customer, I suspect profitably, by buying a backpack for $300, $295. Since then, I have purchased another backpack. I have purchased three other bags. I've purchased a sandbag. I've purchased a couple of shirts, pants. So I did that probably over three plus months after I became a customer with GoRuck. So is that 85% of the revenue? Is it exactly how it breaks down for me? Maybe not, it's probably more than that actually. So if you have a good product, good experience for people in an e-commerce environment, and it's not like a one-off purchase where you only need to buy it once, or it's not a commodity purchase where who cares where they get it kind of thing. The other example I used is AwayTravel.com. I bought myself something after seeing one of their ads. I bought something for myself, and I then bought something for my wife, and I've since bought something else again for myself. So that purchase was fairly recently. So once I became a customer, and it was a good product, if it's a product that lends itself to buying more, and there are more options, and you're thinking about more ways for me to buy them, I would expect yes, that significantly more of your revenue will be later. However, for e-commerce, I would still use Roaz as the indicator. Make money on every sale, unless it's like super inexpensive, then that's hard. But in general for e-commerce, Roaz is a better indicator. And try to make 48 times is really that's the range you're gonna wanna be in, if possible. Less than that, then you just have to figure out your economics. All right, next question, module zero. I talked about CPA, cost per acquisition, cost per lead. Are the metrics the same? Absolutely not. Do each of these metrics have specific nuances? Absolutely yes. Cost per acquisition is the amount of money you spend to acquire a customer. Someone who has paid you money. Cost per lead is the amount of money that you have spent to get somebody to opt in to be on your email list. They do not have to have purchased. You can have, and I have worked with clients, you have had lists in literally the mid to upper, nearly a million people on them. And they, getting money out of those lists was like blood from a stone. Lead, cost per lead independently of any of other factors is a meaningless metric. You can optimize for leads. And the enormous direct response, marketing companies often have, I mean, their conversion rates are staggeringly low because they optimize for cost per lead. How inexpensively can we get a lead? So I don't like CPL as a metric at all. You can do some, you can make cost per lead a little more valuable. One of my friends, Jeremy Blossom at Strait Point Media, has kind of an interesting metric. They do high volume lead gen, they're exceptionally good at it. But he is a metric called cost per quality lead. And that has, they have some other factors that go into that. And they build their system around optimizing for quality leads. So nuance, but the question here is CPL, CPL is saying not at all. You can have a million leads and not a single customer. And if you're optimizing to tell your friends how big your list is, CPL is important. If you're optimizing to put money in the bank and have the life that you wanna lead, CPL is a lot more important. Getting people to buy is hard. And to know how much it costs you to get people to buy is a critical, absolutely critical. I just missed that fast. All right. If a team works for the homework to modules one, module one, two questions about direction strategy. What answers? I'm not actually reading this to myself. So if a team works for the homework to module one, two questions about direction and strategy. What answer should they choose? The execution guys answers, the managers answers, the owners, oh, okay. All right. So the funny dynamics here, right? So who is the decision maker around direction and strategy, right? So who gets to set, that's dependent on the client. The decision maker is the decision maker. So if you're the decision maker, excellent. If you're not, you're kind of beholden to whoever the decision maker is. But the phrase I use a lot is, is the decision intellectually defensible? Not is it right or is it wrong? We don't know until we update it. But when we make a decision, can we make the decision? And when we describe out loud why we've made the decision, is it defensible? Because I, and I, this phrase came back from, for me really arose out of being in too many meetings where there were six or seven different things being discussed. And then the decision maker said, well, we're going to do it this way, period. Or I think it's more, it's going to be at whatever it is. And it was, what's great that you think that, but why do you think that? And they're like, well, I just think that. And this is, or this is the way it's going to be. It's like, that's not, and it's, this is how to win friends with clients. Like, you know, telling the person who's the decision maker in the room that the way they just described something is not intellectually defensible, is not something I'm going to recommend in the client services module. You have to have a hell of a relationship with a client to do that. But everybody around the table answering these questions needs to be able to make, to answer the questions in ways that make sense, not just, well, I think it's going to be X. That's, I think the answer to the question. Anything on that, Andre, that you want to, if you understood it differently that I did? No, I think that's right. I can't remember where, I've heard it a few times. It might have been, may have been Ray Delio, or somebody else who have said something similar with, when they're in the around table meetings, you know, everyone's decision, including the big boss, the decision maker, they need to be able to justify why they, why they picking a certain side, you know. Yeah, but I don't remember where it is from, but yeah, that's exactly the way I'll go. Yeah, and there's a really good conversation with, Tim Ferriss and Josh Waits, and Josh Waits was a elite chess player, incredible thinker, he was the subject for the movie, Searching for Bobby Fisher. He talks about, I think it was Magnus Carlson, was one of the great chess players, the greatest of the greats, who would analyze his, he wrote a book about chess, imagine that, and what he talked about is, after every game, he would, it wasn't about whether he won the game or he lost the game, he would go through his decision making about why did he choose to make the decisions that he made, regardless if he won or not, because you can make bad decisions and still win against an inferior opponent. And this is critical for our thinking too, when we sit around the table and have a conversation about what initiatives we're gonna do or we think about it ourselves, the more we externalize why we're making the decision, even if it's just a little bolded list with pencil, like I think X is going to happen because of this, this, this, and this, or whatever it is. And then when X happens, don't just say, oh, that's, I'm a genius, I was right. Go check and see, did it happen because of the way, and maybe you can't find, like you don't know, and it's impossible, okay, I'm gonna be honest, but I was like a crazy, but it's not the outcome of our decision that's as impactful as the decision-making process that goes into making the decision, because that's where we have control over it. You can get lucky 10 times in a row and not realize you're being lucky 10 times in a row. And then all of a sudden at 11th time, you lose it all because you've relied on a decision-making model that was not sound. So, and this doesn't have to be complicated, just when you're about to do, if you're about to do with an A, B test or whatever, you're about to do something, just take a position. I think X is going to happen because of Y. And the position might be stupid. Like, I might do a headline test and say, I think letter case is going to outperform sentence case because every time I've run this test in the past, that's been true. Like, okay, well, that's generous. That's not really what we're getting. We're getting more at the, I'm gonna test this angle in an ad versus this one. And I think this one's going to outperform because I think this is really the felt need of the audience, not this. And then the data comes back and can confirm or disconfirm. Or maybe neither, maybe you just don't know. But just get into that habit of taking a position. Okay, that's a really important concept is to just internalize why something happened. You know, just go back and reflect on it and try and figure out if it all possible. Tyler Cohen said something similar because he also plays chess. And regardless of if he won a game, he would go back and analyze because he knew that he had made some bad decisions and he was just lucky to have won whatever the game was. So, and that's the only way to improve, not through luck. Right, nothing's true. All right, let's see here. Now, one thing to add to that question though, that's just really important. Everything that we do is as, when I say we, I mean the collective, we all of us who are in this endeavor and those who are in the same endeavor outside of this cohort, there is science, there is probability, there's all of that, but there's art too. And there's a lot of art. So, what I just described is a very analytical process, rational way to approach things. But, I'm very sensitive to this phenomenon where people tell you something like it's sort of canonical truth, yet they don't actually do it that way. So, from a client perspective and sort of an analysis perspective and other things, what I just described you is how I approach things. But, if I go back four or five weeks, whenever it was when Andre and I first wrote that, the very first email on the Tuesday that went out that began to discuss the traffic engine, we weren't sitting around talking about the experiment for today's email, right? It wasn't, that was art. That, the broad experiment in that was, if I sit down every day and write something for tomorrow with all my heart and soul that I think is valuable, and I give it to Andre and he does the same, we released that out into the world, we weren't thinking like, okay, well, what's the rationale here for that? That's just how we did it. And now, if we got a bunch of feedback back, there was like you two are idiots, and what do you say? Well, okay, we might course correct. But, we took a position and we did it in the, and as we got feedback, that feedback suggested the position we took was the right one. So, but that wasn't science. That wasn't analysis and statistics. So, it's not always that. And I can't stress enough that even though we have all this data available to us, and we can look at 12 different attribution models and confuse ourselves six ways from Sunday, that doesn't mean that's always how to make decisions. Sometimes the right decision to make is, I believe in my heart of hearts that this is what's most valuable to this audience, and I am going to go down this road with all of my being and see what happens. Like sometimes that's just the answer. Now, if you get halfway down that road and everyone tells you you're an idiot, okay, maybe course correct. Maybe you took it a little bit, but if they tell you you're an idiot in the Facebook comment, don't worry about it, because that's a different follow-up, that doesn't count. Okay, in our business model, Julie Connors, they harvest important money at the first sell the front end, and then they might double income client in 10 to 20 years from supplies. So consumables presumably. Most of the products are of this kind. Should we use the e-commerce model or a direct response? I use e-commerce, use ROAS. You have a continuity model. So you just know you're good. I mean, you can, love when I answer a question and I'm about to contradict myself. I guess you both, right? That's so entertaining, right? This is true. I mean, I think the better answer is probably use both and see which one gives you the more actionable insights to use to make your business better. So ROAS is going to tell you that you made X amount of money on this first sale. That's a piece of the information. But if you know that the lifetime value from continuity from consumables is significant, then you're really less interested. So you might take a 4X increment in ROAS versus 8, because you know you're making all this money over time. So it's just, it's nuance. I mean, I would just want to know, I would want to know lifetime value, and I would want to know ROAS, and I would want to just kind of see those things and then you just have a good picture of your business. I think that's my near final answer until I change my mind again. Okay, this must have been disconnected. In this case, what can I do for better integration of principle five launch final? That must be disconnected for something. That don't seem like it's missing something. Do you understand that better than I do, Andre? No. And I've deleted the first part of the question. All right, so we'll just jump down to the next one. You can re-ask that question if you want to and clarify. Yeah. All right, where does YouTube fit in? Seems like it's more like Facebook interruption model, but I wonder if we use these keywords like Google. I didn't include YouTube in the traffic engine because it's enough of its own thing, and it's different enough that I just don't know. I think it muddies the waters, so I am going to do the right thing. Someone else asked this in the comments about YouTube, and I'm gonna put together a list of YouTube resources and just share that independently, but this traffic engine is not YouTube, and I don't want to go down that road because I'm scared of misleading people. It's different enough, and I'm less knowledgeable about it than I should be talking about. Okay, I'm curious about reconciling or synergies what we personally want to optimize in business results and what we're wanting to optimize for the user. As in the result experience, I'm wanting to optimize for my clients as different from my personal goals from my business. So yeah, this is a fascinating question. So in an ideal world, more of, this is actually, I think the answer to this, Jared, is to go read Jim Collins' work on the flywheel. You can buy, there's a monograph, which is an excerpt, I believe, from Good to Great, where he talked about the flywheel concept. You can get it on Kindle, it's just a little 50-page monograph. There's a Jim Collins interview with Tim Ferris, and he really goes into detail about the flywheel, which is very, it's profound, right? And it's really easy to get wrong, and the thing to understand about the flywheel when you look at those two resources and get it is that each step of the flywheel needs to lead invariably to the next. It's not just, I've seen people use this concept wildly wrong and teach it wildly wrong. Like it's just four things you do one after the other, and it makes this result. It's not what Jim Collins is talking about. He was talking about that it's four things that naturally lead to one another, and they can't but help to lead to one another, and it's that momentum created by that natural energy that creates the flywheel. So the way that fits into your question is if there's something that you're doing that you're optimizing for yourself, and it doesn't lead invariably to the outcomes that your clients want or your customers want, that little bit of disconnect is when to create a little bit of friction in your flywheel, and that what I think you want to do instead is figure out, and it doesn't mean do a different business or serve a different audience. It just means take a step back and figure out, can you sequence things so that when you sequence them, that you reduce the friction. So that what looks like it's, it could be providing a different type of value, a different methodology of that, and there are lots of ways to do it. But I think that framework is really going to help you answer this question, because when it works, it should feel effortless. Like for me, my flywheel, I sort of spent some time thinking about this, a lot of time thinking about it, I got it wrong enough times, and I think now that I have it right, it feels very right. So for me, I am really, really curious, and I'd like to learn, and because of that, I tend to consume a lot of information, and when I consume a lot of information, at some point I want to understand it. And in order to understand it, I have to sort of summarize it and make sense of it, and that leads me to, and I share it with other people. It's sort of a natural thing that I do. And when I share that with other people, what happens is those other people are like, oh right, that reminds me of this, this, this, and this, and that feeds my curiosity, which starts the whole thing over again. That's why it's a flywheel. That each step leads invariably. And if I do something different than that, it slows me down. But when I'm in that groove, life is good in what I produce is effortless, and it's just everything is better. So that little bit of friction that you have identified is actually more friction than you can imagine when it's compounded over the next, however many decades of your career. So I would spend the time now to have them, to put them, to construct a mechanism, a conceptual mechanism in your life that when you are at your absolute best, is where, when you organize yourself that way, is where you are producing the greatest value for your audience. And when you get those two things in sync, however you have to structure that, I think you're gonna find your results just hockey stick. Andre, anything on that one? Yeah, I think the flywheel is an amazing resource. And the best answer for that, definitely get both, get the monograph and then listen to the Ferris interview. In any order, I guess, I'm not sure one needs to come before the other, but consume them both, and you'll get enough depth to be able to then do it yourself. Paul. All right, next question, Stephen. Okay, Jared, let's see. Clarity, this was on the previous thing, they're not incompatible, we can see point, which yes, I answered that one already. Fly, will fly, will fly, let's answer. All right, Tom, so is it more cost efficient to keep your attractive traffic on Facebook for the first player, filtering pre-sale pre-frame for setting up on Facebook pages, but then on chat bot, et cetera, or is it cheaper than directing the way to an external medium? So the answer is, there's a broader answer, it's not a yes, no answer. So I think it is better, in my experience suggests strongly that it is better to pre-qualify as much as possible in the Facebook ad. It's just the difference of approach, this is really what the manifesto was about, it was really where the traffic engine came from, which is the old model of traffic was based on volume, which was get people away from the traffic medium, the traffic platform, and as fast as possible to a landing page, and let the landing page do the heavy lifting. And then that model is slightly different where it was get people out of the traffic platform as quickly as possible to a squeeze page to get the lead and then sell them by email. Those are sort of the old models, and those models only worked because traffic was cheap. Now traffic is not cheap, in fact traffic is expensive. So rather than use that volume-based approach, the filtering, especially on Facebook in my opinion, just by the nature of Facebook, the filtering happens more in that platform. And is it because it's within the platform? No, that to me is not really the thing here. But I mean, I'm sure there's some credibility around the fact that reading something within the confines of this, of the Facebook as a brand entity, sure, like all of that's probably true, but that's not really what we care about. What we care about is that we're having an honest conversation that is not trying to sell the click. And I realized saying that probably, like a lot of you have stopped me like, wait a minute, isn't the entire job of the ad to sell the click? And I don't believe it is anymore. I believe the job of the ad is to, as much to discourage the click from low value traffic as it is to encourage, not encourage to attract the click from the right person. And that's why we spend more time with longer copy, really framing out an idea that they can consume the idea without leaving Facebook before giving us the signal that they're interested in more instead of saying, essentially, hey, can you believe so and so? Got a picture of her with such and such, click bait, right? That's not the game we're in. We really, from a conceptual perspective, a Facebook ad is holding up a sign that says just as much, don't click here if any of the following is true, as it does click here if the following is true, right? We're not looking for a vacuum cleaner of sucking lead clicks, and we are really trying to distinguish. Yeah, so that's specific to Facebook. It's not true for AdWords, right? I mean, it is sort of true in AdWords, but AdWords is a different animal, Google ads. It's a different animal now because that person's looking for something. So yes, the ad needs to speak to what they're looking for, but we don't need them to spend three hours reading the ad and verify that they might be interested because the fact that they searched has shown that they're interested. We just need to make it clear that they should be interested in us. So that's the distinguishing character. Okay, Marco, let's see, Marco, what do we have here? All right, Francis asks, how do these concepts and methods apply to breaking more of businesses like plumbers, dental offices? I'm sure somebody's gonna send me a message like, oh no, we did it, right? But just go with me. So some things, they don't really fit conceptually. However, if you were a plumber, dental offices are a little different because dental offices are often a little more like a direct response model than they want to let on. It massages a little bit different. The most like a direct response model is a dental office because they have a front end product. That front end product is teeth cleaning. They have some midpoint, knee-based products which are fillings and other stuff. And then they have an incredible back end of teeth lightening, implants, I mean, all kinds of high end stuff. So dental offices are a little bit different. I think on the front end, when I say the front end, on the front end of their business, it's more need space, right? You go to the dentist because you have the need usually for whatever that front end thing is, teeth cleaning and other stuff or you have crack of tooth or whatever. So that part's a little bit different. But dental offices, this applies to a lot. So one of the reasons there are so many specialties related to helping build your dental office and other things are chiropractors and others. There's sort of subsets in that field that they're more like direct response. They have a big group of service providers trying to approach them. Plumbers and massage and other things like that. Conceptually, you still need to understand how do you acquire new customers, right? And the way, and you may acquire new customers entirely through word of mouth. And if you have enough customers, check the box, you're done, you've done it. A lot of plumbers get most of their customers because they work with contractors and they do the installation for contractors and the contractor is the horse they ride, right? And that works, that works too. Massage is a little different. That's, you know, people would often search for massage therapists also word of mouth. So yes, the concepts apply broadly speaking, but no, don't try to turn your massage therapy business into a direct response webinar funnel business. Please, the world doesn't need that. So I look at total sales and move tiny bits inside and see what happens. So, I don't know if you're asking me about my business system or about clients, so I'll do both quickly. So I have, there's sort of two factors that I've, keep in mind, I've been doing this a long, long, long time. I didn't have this, they didn't, if you asked me this question 15 years ago, this is not the answer you're going to get. There was a lot of trial and error, mostly error. So, I optimize my, my business now really, I overweight experience versus outcome. So, I don't, this, I didn't realize this until quite recently. I don't really like money, and that's going to sound weird. And I do have some expensive habits like cars, but I am not money motivated and I don't like looking at financial reports. And I don't like any of that. My, my relationship with money is a concept I borrowed from Dan Sullivan, the founder of strategic coach, which is I don't want money to be the reason I don't do something. So, no, I have a, I'm into German automobiles. I could spend $160,000 on an R8. I'm not going to, and, but I also know that money is not the reason I'm not going to do that. I get a pile of money sitting around and still not going to buy an R8. So, that's sort of how I approach money. So, I overleap my system and I optimized my system really around, primarily around the fact that I like to do interesting things with interesting people. So, I overvalue the opportunities to do that. And as long as the money is at a level that's not a problem, I'm perfectly okay. And I don't want to think about money. I'm not a money optimizer. Client, so how do I optimize? How do I actually do the work of optimization? You know, I've, the single greatest tool for me to do that is morning pages. I have morning pages every day for over 40 years. And I have learned more about sort of, I can't, or I cannot recommend morning pages more strongly, do it. And then you decide you quit because it's too hard, shut up and do it again. And then just keep doing it because it's the single greatest thing that you can do to figure out what matters to you and what really matters and how that's important. So, that's how I optimize my system. I let my subconscious reveal itself and I have these insights that I'm amazed by. And then I continually make my process a little bit better in that direction as I understand myself more. From a client perspective, how do we optimize client systems? The biggest lever that I have with clients is getting them to understand what it is they're actually trying to accomplish. And it is shocking to me how few people I've worked with over the years can tell me in 30 seconds or less what they're actually trying to accomplish. Some can. I have a client who owns a resort in the day one first meeting with that client. I said, what, you know, the basics about the business. They said, the only thing you need to understand is that your job is to put heads and beds. That's it. That's what makes this business work. People, you know, if we don't have heads and beds, nothing else matters. Perfect. That's clarity. Most people can't do that. So, the first thing that I do with clients is I ask them and get them to that perspective. And not all of them will do it. I'm wondering if Lauren's thinking of the same example. We had a client recently where it was just we were chasing butterflies. You know, you just do a little bit of work in one direction and we're working another direction. It's kind of like, what are we doing this for? And the thing we thought we were doing it for wasn't why we were doing it. So, sometimes it's hard to nail that down. But once you have that, all of the decisions are subordinated to that outcome. And that's the hardest thing to understand about systems theory. You're like, well, look, over there, that thing is not doing, it could be better. That could be prettier. That could be whatever. And if that's not the thing that's holding the whole system back, making it better doesn't make the system better. And it's just wasted effort. So, it's this ability to step back and see, like here's what we're optimizing for. Here are the boundaries of the system. Here's like everything where the effect comes from. And then what are the big movers in there? What really makes this happen? With the resort coin I mentioned before, the big mover was external to the system. When the weather is right and it snows and the ski areas are wildly attractive, then that external factor drives the internal system. And I didn't have control over that. We could just ride that wave. Long answer. Anything, any thoughts on this? Well, your product may be different. Someone's going to send me an email and say, bro, that's not true. I do X, Y, I, okay, I get that. But broadly speaking, consumer based, whether it's travel or other things in the United States statistically speaking, you're more likely to be speaking to a woman, significantly more likely to be speaking to a woman for decision making. So, that's the question that across phenomenal number of clients, even with clients where the person they speak to initially is a man, the decision maker that is for the final decision maker, often in those situations is a woman. That's just the nature of reality. But the broader answer is yes, I mean, you want to speak as closely to the person who you know is going to be making the decision as possible. If you can, if you can't figure it out, just don't be take a position, like right to somebody always right to somebody. So, if you think for whatever reason that the most likely buyer is a woman speak to her in your copy, and if your copy doesn't work and if things are going sideways, speak to him, see what happens. And last question that looks like Latin America is a market with a mentality of make money now. I want to build a business focus on customers with a different mindset, or long term business my biggest problem is tracking those customers with ads. What point should I improve my campaign to generate higher quality leads. The question I would ask yourself is, do you really want to fight against that trend. If the if the market that you're in the mentality of that market primarily is make money now would be much easier to sell make money now to that market. Now, if make money now isn't the reality. And it's that's hard and it's not going to work. I know that they think they want make money now but but that's not actually whatever reason that's not the right thing is it's very difficult to meet somebody at the very beginning and say, Hey, this thing that I know you want is wrong. What you really should want is this. That's hard. What you need to do instead of what I would recommend you do instead is meet them where they are, acknowledge that you understand that show empathy. And then you can say, you know, you can say, you know, you can say, I want to get back to them that you know what they're really interested in, and then start the journey from there, then bring them into your world from that starting point where you're building on some trust from that empathy that you can say, I wanted it you want it, whatever, but it's not that simple. Here's why it's not that simple. This this and this. And let me explain this this and this, and then you take them on a journey, and that by the time you get farther along that journey, you have taken them from where they were to where you want them to be it's a little harder. And that rapport is worth it. And it's worth doing that. If the predominant vibe in your market is something that you're, you need to work in your favor. Yes, I Buckminster Fuller quote, use forces don't work. Don't work against forces use them. You try to work against the over like an overarching mentality, it's too hard recognize it, and then show them a better way, and the people who don't want the better way or they'll go away you'll filter them out. But I think you'll on balance capture far more people by showing that empathy and by saying, listen, I get it, I want that to. And I wanted that to like, I totally get it.
